old_claim.note: Printing Press is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim_absent: No printing dependency remains because the scoped system is paid admission and seating allocation at public playhouses, which operated through prices and venue zones without requiring printed tickets.

source_shape.source_locator: The Globe documents one-penny yard admission and higher prices for benches, cushions, and premium rooms without identifying printed tickets as part of the admission mechanism.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Commercial theater admission used tiered payment and spatial seating categories inside public playhouses.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The mechanism is payment for venue access and seat class; a Gutenberg Bible citation proves printing technology but does not establish it as a dependency of theater admission.

ontology_before: Commercial theater admission was tied to the printing press through an unrelated Gutenberg Bible source.
ontology_after: Commercial theater admission depends on public playhouse infrastructure, while printing remains separate publicity and publishing context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: printing_press is no longer a direct prerequisite for theater_ticketing_systems.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to a documented printed-ticket issuing system rather than paid venue admission.
A source established printed tickets as necessary to the earliest scoped London playhouse admissions.
short_reason: Tiered playhouse admission did not require printed tickets.
